title: 1. What is HTML?
content:
Welcome to web development! HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages. Think of it as the skeleton that gives every website its structure. It uses 'tags' to define elements like headings, paragraphs, and links.

Every HTML document starts with a <!DOCTYPE html> declaration, followed by an <html> tag. Inside, the <head> holds metadata (like the page title), and the <body> holds all the visible content.
initialCode:
Hello, World!

This is my first paragraph.

title: 2. Text & Headings
content:
Headings are defined with <h1> to <h6> tags. <h1> is the most important (and usually largest) heading, while <h6> is the least. Search engines use headings to index the structure of your web pages.

Paragraphs are created with the <p> tag. You can also use tags like <strong> for bold text and <em> for italicized text to add emphasis.
initialCode:
Main Title

Subtitle

This is a standard paragraph. You can make text bold or italic for emphasis.

title: 3. Links & Images
content:
Links (hyperlinks) are created with the <a> (anchor) tag. The href attribute is crucial—it specifies the destination URL.

Images are embedded using the <img> tag. It's a self-closing tag and requires the src attribute for the image path and the alt attribute for accessibility (it provides a text description if the image can't be loaded).
initialCode:
Links & Images

This is a link to a helpful resource:

MDN Web Docs

Here is an image:

title: 4. Lists (Unordered & Ordered)
content:
HTML offers two main list types. An unordered list (<ul>) is for items where the order doesn't matter, typically shown with bullets. An ordered list (<ol>) is for items where sequence is important, shown with numbers.

In both cases, each list item is defined with an <li> tag.
initialCode:
Unordered List: Shopping List

  • Coffee
  • Tea
  • Milk

Ordered List: Top 3 Planets

  1. Earth
  2. Mars
  3. Jupiter

title: 5. Tables
content:
Tables are used to display data in rows and columns. A table starts with a <table> tag. Each row is defined with <tr>, table headers with <th>, and data cells with <td>.

Adding a simple border with CSS (as shown) makes tables much easier to read.
initialCode:
Employee Data

First Name Last Name
John Doe
Jane Smith

title: 6. Forms & Inputs
content:
Forms are used to collect user input. The <form> element is a container for different types of input elements, such as text fields, checkboxes, radio buttons, and submit buttons.

The <label> tag defines a label for many form elements, which improves accessibility. The for attribute of the label should be equal to the id of the input element.
initialCode: Contact Us

title: 7. Semantic HTML
content:
Semantic HTML elements clearly describe their meaning to both the browser and the developer. This improves accessibility and SEO.

Examples include <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, and <footer>. They function like <div> elements but provide more context about your content.
initialCode:
My Website

Home | About

Main Content

This is where the primary content goes.

Copyright 2025

title: 8. What is CSS?
content:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language we use to style an HTML document. It describes how HTML elements should be displayed on screen. You can add CSS directly into HTML using the <style> tag, typically placed in the <head>.

A CSS rule has a selector (e.g., h1) and a declaration block (e.g., { color: blue; }).
initialCode:
My First Styled Page

This page has some basic styling!

title: 9. Selectors (Class & ID)
content:
To style specific elements, we use class and ID selectors.

  • Class Selector: Use a period (.) followed by the class name (e.g., .highlight). Classes are reusable and can be applied to many elements.
  • ID Selector: Use a hash (#) followed by the id name (e.g., #header). IDs must be unique to a single element on the page.
initialCode:
A Unique Title

This is a paragraph with highlighted text.

This entire paragraph is highlighted.

title: 10. The Box Model
content:
Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The CSS box model describes this concept. It consists of: margins, borders, padding, and the actual content.

  • Content: The text, image, etc.
  • Padding: Transparent space between the content and the border.
  • Border: A line that goes around the padding and content.
  • Margin: Transparent space outside the border, separating it from other elements.
initialCode:
The Box Model in Action

This element has margin, border, and padding.

title: 11. Colors & Backgrounds
content:
CSS gives you full control over colors. Use color for text and background-color for the background of an element.

Colors can be specified by name (red), HEX codes (#ff0000), or RGB values (rgb(255, 0, 0)). HEX is most common for web design.

You can also set a background-image using a URL.
initialCode:
Dark Theme Design

This is a panel with a semi-transparent background color.

title: 12. Typography
content:
CSS gives you control over the appearance of text. Key properties include:

  • font-family: Specifies the font for an element (e.g., 'Arial', sans-serif).
  • font-size: Sets the size of the text.
  • font-weight: Sets how thick or thin characters in text should be displayed.
  • text-align: Aligns the text in an element (left, right, center).
  • line-height: Specifies the space between lines of text.
initialCode:
Mastering Typography

Good typography makes a website easier to read and more visually appealing. You can control many aspects of text, like creating custom styles for emphasis.

title: 13. The Display Property
content:
The display property is one of the most important in CSS. Key values include:

  • block: The element starts on a new line and takes up the full width available (e.g., <p>, <div>).
  • inline: The element does not start on a new line and only takes up as much width as necessary (e.g., <span>, <a>).
  • inline-block: Like inline, but you can set a width and height.
  • none: The element is completely removed from the page.
initialCode:
Home About Contact

The navigation links above use 'inline-block'.

You can't see this paragraph!

title: 14. Positioning
content:
The position property specifies the type of positioning method used for an element. Values include:

  • static: Default value. Elements render in order, as they appear in the document flow.
  • relative: The element is positioned relative to its normal position.
  • absolute: The element is positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor.
  • fixed: The element is positioned relative to the viewport, which means it always stays in the same place even if the page is scrolled.
  • sticky: A hybrid of relative and fixed positioning.
initialCode:
This container is the positioning context for the blue box.

title: 15. Introduction to Flexbox
content:
Flexbox is a modern layout model for arranging items in a single dimension (a row or a column). To use it, create a 'flex container' by setting an element's display property to flex.

Use justify-content for horizontal alignment and align-items for vertical alignment on the container. Try changing their values to center or space-between.
initialCode:
Item 1
Item 2
Item 3

title: 16. Introduction to Grid
content:
CSS Grid is a powerful 2-dimensional layout system, allowing you to control rows and columns simultaneously. Start by setting display: grid on a container.

Use grid-template-columns to define the number and size of columns. The fr unit represents a fraction of the available space, which is very useful for flexible layouts. Use gap to set the space between grid items.

title: 17. Pseudo-classes & Elements
content:
Pseudo-classes are keywords added to selectors that specify a special state of the selected element(s). For example, :hover applies a style when you move your mouse over an element.

Pseudo-elements are keywords added to a selector that lets you style a specific part of the selected element(s). For example, ::before creates a pseudo-element that is the first child of the selected element.
initialCode:
This is a paragraph. The first line of this text will be bold and green. The rest of the paragraph will be normal.

title: 18. Transitions
content:
CSS transitions allow you to change property values smoothly (over a given duration). To create a transition effect, you must specify two things: the CSS property you want to add an effect to, and the duration of the effect.

In the example, we are transitioning the background-color and transform properties over 0.3 seconds.

title: 19. Responsive Design & Media Queries
content:
Responsive web design makes your web pages look good on all devices (desktops, tablets, and phones). This is done using CSS Media Queries.

A media query applies a block of CSS only if a certain condition is true (e.g., the browser viewport is a certain width). The example below changes the flex direction from row to column on small screens, making the layout stack vertically.
